Skip to contents
nlmixr2libingest 0.0.0.9000
  • Reference
    • Ingesting four decades of population-PK literature: challenges and a token-efficient, quality-preserving pipeline

Ingesting four decades of population-PK literature: challenges and a token-efficient, quality-preserving pipeline

nlmixr2 project

Draft — started 2026-06-22

Source: vignettes/articles/ingestion-challenges.Rmd
ingestion-challenges.Rmd

Abstract

Reproducing a published population pharmacokinetic / pharmacodynamic (popPK/PD) model in executable form is a deceptively hard information-extraction problem. The source corpus spans roughly four decades, multiple modelling tools and notational traditions, and no enforced reporting standard. We describe nlmixr2libingest, tooling that lets a large-language-model agent extract such models into the nlmixr2lib library at scale while holding a strict quality invariant: every estimated value and every nonstandard structural equation in the final model is source-traced to the original paper. We frame the cost of agentic extraction (dominated by cached-context re-reads across a build–check–fix loop, not by the paper), catalogue the heterogeneity that makes extraction hard, and present a deterministic-first architecture — log filtering, register lookup, structural archetypes, and optional local-LLM distillation — whose every acceleration is a cheaper input or a prior, never a gate on what the agent reads of the paper.

1. Introduction

Population PK/PD models are published as a mix of prose, equations, tables, and (sometimes) control streams. A reader who wants to run the model must recover its compartmental structure, parameterisation, inter-individual and residual variability, covariate relationships, and dosing/observation conventions, then re-express them in a modelling language. Doing this faithfully for one paper is routine expert work; doing it for thousands of papers is an industrial-scale extraction problem with an unusual constraint — the output must be numerically faithful to a fixed ground truth (the paper), so “plausible but wrong” is a failure, not a minor error.

LLM agents can do this extraction, but at a cost structure that is unintuitive: the paper is cheap (a trimmed paper is on the order of 8.6k tokens); the expense is the cached context re-read on every turn of the build–check–fix loop, multiplied by the number of turns. Reducing cost therefore means shrinking the per-turn context, cutting the turn count, and not dispatching the agent at all on the large fraction of indexed documents that are not fittable popPK papers.

The central design tension is that the cheapest way to shrink context — letting a retrieval system or a small model decide what the agent sees — is exactly the way to silently lose fidelity. Our resolution is a quality firewall: cheap pre-processing may propose and prioritise, but the agent always verifies the final numbers and nonstandard equations against the full paper, which is never gated.

2. The heterogeneity problem (why this is hard)

Notes toward a taxonomy of what varies across the corpus. Each item is a concrete extraction hazard, not just trivia.

  • No standardised reporting. There is no enforced equivalent of CONSORT for popPK. Structural model, parameter table, variability model, and covariate equations may each be in text, a table, a figure, an appendix, or only in a supplementary control stream.
  • Tooling and notation drift over ~40 years. NONMEM (ADVAN/TRANS macros, THETA/ETA/EPS), Monolix (mlxtran), ADAPT, WinNonlin, SAAM II, Phoenix, Stan, and hand-derived closed-form solutions all appear. The same model can be written as ODEs, as a closed-form linCmt()-style solution, or as ADVAN subroutine references the reader must expand.
  • Parameterisation ambiguity. Clearance/volume vs micro-rate constants; CL/V vs k/V; absorption as first-order ka, transit chains, or zero-order; central volume reported as V, V1, Vc, or V/F. Apparent (/F) vs absolute parameters are frequently implicit.
  • Variability conventions. IIV reported as variance, SD, CV%, or %; on log, logit, or additive scale; full vs block vs diagonal Ω; shrinkage sometimes conflated with estimates. Residual error as additive, proportional, combined, or log-normal, with units that must be inferred.
  • Units and scaling. Mixed time units (h vs day), amount vs concentration, molar vs mass, allometric reference weights left unstated, BSA/FFM/IBW derivations (DuBois/Mosteller, Janmahasatian, Devine) often uncited.
  • Covariate model expression. Centering references, fixed vs estimated exponents, indicator-variable encodings, and study-specific covariates whose meaning is not portable across papers (the motivation for a scope field in the covariate register).
  • Document quality. Older papers are scanned images requiring OCR; tables break across pages; Greek letters and subscripts are mangled; errata and supplements live in separate files.
  • Non-fittable look-alikes. GastroPlus/PBPK-QSP exports, posters, reviews, and methods papers are textually similar to fittable popPK papers but should not be dispatched for extraction.

3. Methods (the pipeline)

3.1 Cost model and quality firewall

We treat per-run cost as cache_read × output, where cache_read is roughly (cached context size) × (number of agent turns). The optimisation targets are therefore (a) per-turn context size, (b) turn count, and (c) the share of dispatches spent on non-fittable papers. Every mechanism below is admissible only under the firewall of Section 1: source-trace every final ini() value and any nonstandard model() equation; never gate the paper; treat machine outputs as priors.

3.2 Deterministic levers (no LLM required)

  • Log filtering. devtools::check() and vignette-render logs are reduced deterministically to ERROR/WARNING/NOTE steps and failing chunks. Determinism is the point: an LLM summary that drops a real error would corrupt the loop.
  • Register lookup. The three nlmixr2lib canonical-name registers (covariate ≈284k tokens, compartment, parameter) are parsed into a DuckDB full-text index; the agent retrieves the few relevant entries by name, alias, or concept instead of reading the file. The index rebuilds only when a source register changes.
  • Structural features and archetypes. Each existing model is parsed (via its rxUi) into a structural feature vector — compartments, absorption, elimination, PD type, variability, covariates, residual error — augmented with its description/reference/vignette text. Clustering yields an empirical archetype taxonomy, with an explicit other bucket for unique-but-interesting structures that should be flagged rather than forced into a template.

3.3 Optional, pluggable LLM acceleration

When a local LLM is configured, embeddings (nomic-embed-text) drive a fittable classifier and an archetype prior, and a small instruct model emits a structured distillation sheet (parameters, values, units, RSE, IIV, covariate equations, residual error, dosing) that the agent builds from and verifies against. With no LLM, classification falls back to keyword/feature rules and distillation is skipped — the agent reads the trimmed paper directly. The acceleration is never load-bearing for correctness.

3.4 Scale orchestration

A pre-dispatch classifier annotates likely-non-fittable papers and sets an effort tier (annotate-first; auto-skip only after held-out validation). A soft, complexity-tiered budget checkpoints work and escalates to a human via a sidecar question on overrun rather than hard-killing.

4. Results

Dollar figures use the runner’s recorded blended rate (~$1.3/Mtok-total; cost is dominated by cache-read). Token figures for re-runs were recovered from the agents’ own per-message usage transcripts.

4.1 Cost is cache-read × turns

Two original library extractions, from the runner’s complete RunRecords: vedolizumab 3.25M tokens (2.86M cache-read, 23k output), $4.27, ~20 turns; the van Dijkman decline 1.79M tokens (1.54M cache-read), $2.67, 5 attempts. ~96% of tokens — and of cost — are cache reads. Empirically, run cost ≈ rate × Σ_turns(cached context), so the two control variables are per-turn context size and number of turns.

4.2 Blind re-extraction accuracy

Re-extracting four already-curated papers from source alone, then cross-checking against the shipped models and the papers: (i) a 1-cmt linear mAb — faithful, 11/11 values source-traced, and it actually corrected a unit error in the shipped model (the paper reports ng/mL; the curated model used µg/mL, 1000× off in simulation); (ii) a 2-cmt parallel-linear+Michaelis-Menten model with 13 covariates — full structural and numeric match, 31/31 traced (incl. the supplement’s Table S4); (iii) a PK-free algebraic exposure-response PD model — correct structure, but one real error (it used the residual variance 7.09 where the SD 2.66 belongs); (iv) a meta-modelling catalogue of 11 cited models — correctly declined to fabricate, matching the operator’s original deselection. Two value disagreements, one in each direction, both resolved by reading the paper. The judgement-versus-mechanism boundary is real: the model structure and most values extract reliably; unit conventions and variance-vs-SD encodings are where errors hide.

4.3 A firewall limitation

The programmatic source-trace flags ini() values with no supporting number in the paper, but it verifies presence, not slot: the erroneous residual 7.09 read as “found” because that number is in the paper — as the variance. Catching a right-number-wrong-role error still requires reading the labelled column. A role-aware or LLM-cross-checked variant is future work.

4.4 The register-lookup lever (controlled A/B)

Same six-covariate task, two ways, with full transcript telemetry: reading covariate-columns.md (~136k tokens) into context cost 17.6M tokens / $22.84 (peak per-turn context 532k); resolving the same six via lookup_canonical cost 0.47M tokens / $0.62 (peak context 44k) — ~37× cheaper, 12× smaller peak context, for identical answers. The lookup is a strict win when an agent would otherwise load the register.

4.5 What actually dominates cost

Verbose, general-purpose re-extractions cost 5–14× the originals ($41 vs $4.27 for the hard case) — but not because of the register (lookup-capable agents never loaded it; their peak context stayed below the file size). The driver was turn count: 126–267 model turns versus the tuned runner’s ~20. Because cost ≈ context × turns, a ~10× turn inflation produced a ~10× cost inflation. The single correctly-declined case (23 turns) was the only one that matched the original’s cost.

4.6 Supervised A/B on the live runner: the levers in a real extraction

Section 4.4 measured one lever in isolation; here the combined token-minimising skill is measured end-to-end against the production skill on the real dispatch runner, with full RunRecord telemetry (the accurate vehicle the earlier ad-hoc probes lacked). Two skills were compared: the production extract-literature-model and a -lean variant that (i) resolves every canonical covariate/parameter name in one batched pre-brief instead of a lookup per name, and (ii) replaces the separate vignette-render, convention-lint, and R CMD check gates with a single validate_model(level = "full") call. Everything else was held fixed — same two papers, same model (claude-opus-4-7), same effort, one task at a time on a single account, byte-identical prompts except the skill name — and each cell was run in triplicate.

Aside (baseline provenance, for a later detailed write-up): the production skill is extract-literature-model at nlmixr2lib commit dc10fec8 (origin/main at experiment time); the lean variant was committed at 2226ba8f. All per-run figures are re-derived from the per-attempt result events (the authoritative totals claude reports), not the runner’s summary RunRecord, because one attempt’s RunRecord proved unreliable (see below). One run is excluded: easy/lean/1, a complete extraction the output-evidence gate wrongly flagged “no output” because the task neither committed nor declared a deliverable file — a harness-config error, since corrected, not a model failure.

A second run, hard/production/2, first looked anomalous (its RunRecord showed 102 output tokens) but is in fact a real, expensive run and is included: its attempt held three claude invocations (an 81-turn extraction plus two resumes), and the RunRecord captured only the last resume’s per-call usage while the cost ($9.74) was cumulative; from the result events it is a render-loop extraction of 38k output / 11.9M cache-read.

Every individual extraction run (the $0 stop-sequence throttle-aborts that precede some runs are omitted; * = excluded from the summary):

paper skill rep output cache-read total tok cost wall-clock
easy (1-cmt mAb) production 1 22,704 2,477,773 2,632,714 $2.63 568 s
easy (1-cmt mAb) production 2 34,137 1,536,424 1,696,937 $2.41 486 s
easy (1-cmt mAb) production 3 24,313 2,932,387 3,188,931 $3.53 1015 s
easy (1-cmt mAb) lean 1* 27,126 4,714,280 4,888,691 $3.96 1667 s
easy (1-cmt mAb) lean 2 22,578 1,388,453 1,505,667 $1.85 745 s
easy (1-cmt mAb) lean 3 19,444 1,260,787 1,369,686 $1.68 1116 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) production 1 38,898 5,675,022 6,018,958 $5.72 863 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) production 2 38,102 11,872,475 12,366,126 $9.74 1439 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) production 3 29,151 4,506,228 4,689,133 $3.94 695 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) lean 1 35,507 4,869,027 5,033,247 $4.13 2179 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) lean 2 34,360 6,404,502 6,570,678 $4.89 1628 s
hard (2-cmt+MM, 13 cov) lean 3 45,083 7,522,658 7,766,024 $6.13 2569 s

* easy/lean/1 (the gate-failed task) also ran a second complete extraction (run 4: $1.66 / 1.23M total) before being killed; both excluded as a harness-config artifact, not a model failure.

Means / medians over the included runs (cost | cache-read): easy production $2.86 / $2.63 | 2.32M vs lean $1.76 / $1.76 | 1.32M; hard production $6.47 / $5.72 | 7.35M vs lean $5.05 / $4.89 | 6.27M.

The headline is that the lean skill is cheaper on both papers — clearly on the simple model, modestly and noisily on the complex one. On the easy model it cut cost ~38 % (−43 % cache-read), mainly by collapsing several per-name lookups into one batched pre-brief and passing validation in one or two tries. On the hard model lean was ~22 % cheaper by mean (~14 % by median) with less cache-read — but that gap is driven largely by variance: production’s worst run fell into a render-loop and cost $9.74 (the most expensive run in the set), whereas lean’s three runs were more consistent ($4.13–6.13). Per-run turn counts on the hard model were comparable (production ~58, lean ~57). The honest reading is “cheaper-or-comparable and lower-variance on hard,” not a large guaranteed win, because the fragile hard vignette can trigger a render-loop blow-up under either skill.

A wall-clock caveat orthogonal to tokens: lean was ~2× slower on the hard model because its unified validate_model(level = "full") re-runs a whole-package R CMD check plus render on every fix iteration — minutes of subprocess time that costs little in tokens but much in latency. Scoping that validator to the new model alone would remove the latency without giving up the token savings.

4.7 Where the tokens go across the production corpus

Sampling the 322 completed production extractions (all the production skill; cost range $0.40–$41.87, median $1.84) confirms and generalises Section 4.5. Almost all of the spend is cache-read — the agent’s accumulated context (skill + references + paper + the model/vignette under edit + every prior tool result) re-read on each turn — and it scales with the turn count:

run cost turns cache-read render calls
cheapest $0.40 8 0.34M 0
median-ish $1.85 26 1.45M 0
heavy $15.39 86 22.3M 5
worst $41.87 262 71.0M 105

The distribution is heavily right-skewed: most runs converge in a handful of turns and stay cheap, while a small tail explodes. That tail is almost always a vignette-render fix loop — the $41.87 run issued 105 render calls, and the expensive runs all carry high render counts and many large (>10k-token) tool results. Each failed render adds a turn, appends a large render log to the context, and forces the whole growing context to be re-read on every subsequent turn. So the biggest lever on total corpus spend is not the per-turn context (already modest once the 284k register is replaced by lookup) but capping or preventing the render loop — precisely the failure mode the lean skill’s unified validator must be careful not to amplify (Section 4.6).

5. Discussion

The levers in this package are aimed correctly — at per-turn context (register lookup, log filtering, a lean lazily-loaded skill) — and they save real money when triggered (lookup: ~37× on an isolated task). But the dominant cost driver in practice is the number of turns, which is a property of the dispatching agent’s discipline more than of the tooling. The practical conclusion is that the biggest savings come from a tuned, low-turn dispatch (as the production runner already is), with the input-side levers compounding on top.

Several further input-side pre-processing steps follow directly from the cost model and are candidates for the low-turn path: a cheap local-LLM pass that reads the paper, identifies its covariates, and emits a focused, model-specific covariate report (paper name → canonical name/units/reference) so the agent neither loads the register nor spends turns looking covariates up; a single code-based validation step that runs parse, conventions, source-trace, registry-build, check, and vignette-render and returns one terse “Success” or a short list of file-and-line fixes, replacing the verbose logs that otherwise bloat every subsequent turn; and a policy-driven sidecar auto-responder that answers recurring stop-and-ask questions from a vetted policy table on the next tick, so a run continues on a warm cache instead of paying a cold retry. Each keeps the expensive judgement with the agent while moving deterministic or pre-decidable work off the per-turn critical path.

Open limitations remain: the source-trace presence-vs-slot gap above; the portability cost of a local-LLM dependency (mitigated by graceful degradation); the reliance on small local models for distillation, which the source-trace firewall is designed to catch; and the boundary discipline between this ingestion package and the validated library it feeds, which must stay one-directional (consume, never reimplement validation).

6. Conclusion

A token-efficient extraction pipeline need not trade away fidelity. By keeping every acceleration on the input and prior side of a hard source-trace firewall, and by making the LLM optional rather than central, the same deterministic core that lowers cost also keeps the agent honest.

On this page
